# This file is generated to mirror docs/COMMANDS.org (the Emacs-client
# command reference), translated to Neovim keybindings and :Skg*
# Ex commands. nvim/lua/skg/keymaps.lua is the authoritative source
# for every binding named here; if this file and that module disagree,
# keymaps.lua wins. Where COMMANDS.org describes Emacs-only mechanics
# (the minibuffer, magit), the text below is rewritten for the nvim
# equivalents (vim command-line prompts, neogit / plain git-diff
# buffers) rather than translated word for word.
* user-facing skg commands
** :w : :SkgSave
Each time you save, your view is rebuilt.
This is because you can put 'requests' into your view,
in which you request more data,
such as a node's ancestors, or the nodes that link to it.
Since views can overlap,
saving one open view can change another.
For this reason the client will warn you
if you have unsaved edits in one view
and begin to edit another one.
It won't stop you, but the results could be bad.
If you try to save while another skg buffer already has unsaved
edits, you also get a 'Save anyway?' confirmation before the
request is sent.

':w' is the BufWriteCmd on every skg view buffer, so plain saving
works as expected; ':SkgSave' does the same thing as an explicit
Ex command.
** <localleader>dd : deletion
***     <localleader>dd : :SkgDelete
Marks the node at point for deletion from the graph.
Upon saving the view, it will vanish not just from this view,
but from the graph itself.
*** <localleader>dR : :SkgDeleteRecursive
Same as Delete, but also marks every activeNode org-descendent
of the node at point. Descendent headlines that are not activeNodes
(phantoms, aliascol, id-col, etc.) are skipped.

Its own binding, not a prefix-arg variant -- nvim has no prefix-arg
protocol to reuse here the way Emacs's C-u did. Call it directly as
<localleader>dR, or as ':SkgDeleteRecursive'.
** <localleader>f : text search
The 'f' is for 'find'.
*** <localleader>ff : :SkgSearch
Text search across titles and aliases, returning every
matching node. "Rooty" nodes (roots, cycle members, link
dests, and nodes that had an org-roam ID before import)
are bumped in the ranking via their context-origin multiplier.

Prompts for search terms. All search axes take their conservative
defaults: no per-token regex, titles only (no body), no Tantivy
phrase and operator syntax.

Run 'Goto' (<localleader>gg, below) from one of the links in the
search results to open the view from that node.
*** <localleader>fi : :SkgSearchInteractive (search-interactive)
Same as Search, but after the search terms it prompts:

: Type a character for each search option: (r)egex, (b)ody, (t)antivy syntax, (h)elp:

Type one character for each option you want, in any order,
then press Enter. An empty answer uses the same conservative
defaults as ':SkgSearch'. Unrecognized characters are ignored.
Typing =h= opens this documentation (docs/COMMANDS-nvim.org,
falling back to docs/COMMANDS.org if that file is missing), with
all folds opened and the cursor searched onto this headline -- and
abandons the search. If the headline can't be found the whole file
is still shown, with a notice that the search failed.

- =r= (*regex*) — per-token regex instead of phrase search.
  Bypasses the query parser; each whitespace-separated piece
  is handed to Tantivy's 'RegexQuery'.
- =b= (*body*) — also search the body field (titles are
  always searched).
- =t= (*tantivy syntax*) — Tantivy phrase and operator syntax
  (AND/OR/NOT/+/-). In non-regex mode this passes through
  Tantivy's QueryParser (phrase quotes, grouping, field: all
  work; intra-word operator chars get escaped so =foo:bar=
  remains findable). In regex mode, AND / OR / NOT / +foo /
  -bar combine the per-piece RegexQueries at the document
  level; phrase syntax does not apply there. See the regex=true
  section below.
**** Query syntax with default axes (phrase/operators=false, regex=false)
Queries are literal. The query string is split on whitespace,
and any word containing a Tantivy operator character
(=+ - & | ! ( ) { } [ ] ^ " ~ * ? : \=) or the bare keyword
=AND= / =OR= / =NOT= is wrapped as a phrase so its contents
are searched literally. Words without specials pass through.
- *OR between words*. "dog cat" matches titles containing
  either word; a title with both outranks one with only one.
- *Literal special characters*. A title like "C++ tips",
  "[draft] plan", or "cat:dog" is findable by typing its
  literal text.
**** Query syntax with phrase/operators=true
The query is handed to Tantivy's QueryParser, with one
heuristic adjustment: any operator character sitting strictly
inside a word (with word chars on both sides, scanning
through consecutive operators within the same token) is
backslash-escaped. So =foo:bar= stays findable as a literal,
but =AND= / =OR= / =+foo= / =-bar= / ="fox in socks"= /
=(dog OR cat)= keep their operator meaning.

Known imperfection: the heuristic can't distinguish a
configured field name from a user word, so
=title_or_alias:dog= gets its =:= escaped too. Field-
qualified queries need manual =\:= in operators mode.
**** Query syntax with regex=true
The query is split on whitespace and each piece is handed to
'tantivy::query::RegexQuery' as its own pattern. Matches are
against *tokens*, not raw text: the indexer lowercases and
splits on whitespace, so no stored token ever contains a
space. That's why splitting is the only way multi-word regex
queries return anything. Patterns like =histor.*= work for
prefix search ('history', 'historical'), and =polit.* scien.*=
combines two such prefix searches. Combine with body=true to
also search body fields.

The *=*= gotcha*: in regex =*= means "zero or more of the
previous character", NOT "any characters" (that's =.*=). So
=sci*= matches the tokens =sc=, =sci=, =scii=, … — not
"anything starting with sci". Use =sci.*= for that.

With operators=false, pieces are OR'd (SHOULD-combined)
like the non-regex default.

With phrase/operators=true (the wire flag is still
=operators=true=), the keywords =AND= / =OR= / =NOT= and the
prefixes =+piece= / =-piece= combine the per-piece RegexQueries
at the document level:
- =poli.* AND sci.*= — documents whose tokens include one
  matching =poli.*= AND one matching =sci.*=.
- =poli.* NOT bio.*= — documents matching =poli.*= and NOT
  any token matching =bio.*=.
- =+poli.* -bio.*= — same as above, without the keywords.
- =poli.* OR sci.*= — explicit OR, same as the default.
Keywords are consumed rather than matched as patterns. An
=AND= adjacent to a piece on either side promotes that piece
to MUST.
**** Caveats
- Each alias is indexed as its own document, so a query can
  match on an alias even when the title doesn't mention the
  searched word. The returned node is still the aliased one.
- Results are ranked by BM25 times a context-origin factor
  (Root 100×, CycleMember ~32×, Dest 10×, HadID 10×,
  MultiContained ~3.2×, everything else 1×). A weaker text
  match on a root can outrank a stronger match on a leaf,
  but every match is returned — search is never filtered by
  graph role.
- Regex distance is tokenwise: a pattern never spans
  whitespace, because we split the query on whitespace before
  handing the pieces to RegexQuery. A true whole-title regex
  (one pattern that matches across spaces) would need a
  separate non-tokenized =raw_title= field. Not done yet.
*** <localleader>fl : :SkgSearchMakeLink
Search-then-link: pick a result, return here, get a link.

Like ':SkgSearch' but tailored for inserting a link to a node
you don't yet know the ID of. The buffer and cursor position you
called it from are remembered. The same conservative search
defaults as ':SkgSearch' apply.

Unlike the Emacs client, the source buffer is NOT saved before
the search runs -- only its position is recorded. (Saving first
can let the server's rerender collapse a freshly typed blank
line, leaving the remembered position dangling on the next
headline, so the inserted link would glue itself to the wrong
spot.) Save your own edits first if you want them preserved
before detouring into the search.

In the resulting search buffer, find the result you want and
press <CR> (or <localleader>cc). The search buffer closes; you
return to the buffer you started in, at the position you were at;
and a =[[id:X][LABEL]]= link is inserted at point.

The label is the result's title with any inline links inside
it reduced to their labels (so the new link's label is plain
text, never another link inside a link).
** <localleader>g : going to a node
*** <localleader>gg : :SkgGoto
Calling this when the cursor is on a link, a node's metadata,
a bare UUID, or a .skg filename
will open a new view from the ID it finds there,
or go to the view of it that was already open if that exists.

If the node is overridden, this opens the override-choice menu
instead -- the node as root, its overriders beneath it -- and
explains so in the echo area; run Goto on the overrider you
want, or GotoBypassOverride to open the original. From a neogit
buffer or a plain git-diff buffer (=skg://git-diff/...=), Goto
bypasses the menu automatically (a readable-ID jump lands on the
raw node).

If you're not sure whether the cursor is on something visitable
(a link or a node's metadata),
use IdNext and IdPrev (the ]i / [i motions, or <localleader>in /
<localleader>ip)
to identify where those are.
*** :SkgGotoBypassOverride
Like Goto, but if the node is overridden, opens the node
itself instead of the override-choice menu. The escape hatch from
the menu (or from anywhere) to the original node. Recursive
content beneath the opened node still draws overriders in place
of what they override.
*** <localleader>GG : :SkgGotoAndCloseThis
Like Goto, but also closes the buffer it was called from.
*** <localleader>gb : :SkgGotoBiggestBranch
Go to the branch near point with the most recursive content,
estimated by counting org-descendents in the current buffer.

If the node at point has siblings, this considers that sibling
set. If it has no siblings, it considers the node's immediate
children instead.

This can help when a view is loading slowly:
replacing the content relationship to its biggest branch
with a link relationship (<localleader>ml)
reduces the view's demands on the server.
*** <localleader>gi : :SkgGotoById
'Goto' is usually more convenient.

This requires a literal v4 UUID string, like
3861db2c-7f16-4814-b403-52b5e05d5e0a
It can be useful going from git to skg.
*** <localleader>Gi : :SkgGotoByIdAndCloseThis
*** <localleader>gm : :SkgGotoInGit
Opens a neogit status view of the changes to the file containing
the node at point. If the file appears in multiple sections
(unstaged, staged, untracked), point lands on the first and
the user is warned. (If neogit is unavailable, this falls back
to the plain git-diff view described below.)
*** <localleader>Gm : :SkgGotoInGitAndCloseThis
*** <localleader>gM : :SkgGotoInGitParent
Opens a plain git-diff buffer (named =skg://git-diff/FILE=) of
the *parent*'s file -- the combined staged+unstaged diff, or the
untracked file's own content if it has no HEAD diff -- with point
on the first line in that buffer containing this node's ID.
Warns if the ID appears multiple times. (Emacs's magit has an API
for landing on a specific line within a status section; neogit
doesn't, so this variant uses a plain diff buffer instead of a
neogit view -- same information, different chrome.)
*** <localleader>GM : :SkgGotoInGitParentAndCloseThis
** <localleader>t : git operations
*** <localleader>ta         : :SkgGitAddIfNewRecursivePreview
Brings up a buffer (=skg://git-add-new-files=) with the text of a
shell script which, if run, 'git add's some nodes. The nodes
should consist only of nodes new to git, and only of nodes in
the recursive content (inclusive) of the node from which
GitAddIfNewRecursivePreview was called. The buffer has readable
ids enabled.

Unlike the Emacs client, which shows executable elisp you run in
place with C-x C-e, this is a plain shell script: review it, then
run it yourself (copy it into a shell, or write the buffer to a
file and execute that).
*** <localleader>tA         : :SkgGitAddIfNewRecursive
Like GitAddIfNewRecursivePreview,
but it doesn't show you the script;
it instead runs that logic for you directly.
*** <localleader> (g|G)(m|M) : :SkgGotoInGit*
These four commands are described elsewhere in this document.
*** <localleader>tr         : :SkgDiffReport
Asks whether to include staged changes and, if so,
whether to include unstaged changes.
Then opens an org report (=skg://diff-analysis=) summarizing
semantic graph changes across all configured sources.

The report is based on git/index/worktree state,
so the command refuses to run while any Skg view buffer
has unsaved edits.
*** <localleader>tm         : :SkgStageMoves
Scans the git status of every configured source and finds each
node whose .skg file "moved": it vanished from exactly one source
(committed there in git HEAD, now gone from that source's worktree)
and appeared in exactly one other source (present in that source's
worktree, not yet committed). A node that vanished from, or appeared
in, more than one source is ambiguous and is skipped.

Opens a buffer (=skg://stage-moves=) holding a bash script: a list
of the moves followed by one loop that 'git rm's each file in its
old source and 'git add's it in the new one. Before removing, the
loop checks that the file is already gone from the old source's
worktree (that is how the move was detected); a file still present
there is skipped, not deleted, since 'git rm' would silently remove
it. Review the script, then run it from the skg data root. Unlike
most commands here, this one does not depend on the node at point;
it reports moves across all sources.
** <localleader>c : show a collection (both cols of a relation)
These commands don't edit the graph. Each inserts a request into the
metadata of the node at point that, on save, causes the server to
render the relation's collections (cols) into the view, POPULATED from
the graph. Each builds BOTH cols of its relation. The read-only-col ☮
marks a col you cannot change from here. Every command auto-saves.
*** <localleader>ca : :SkgShowCollectionAliases
The aliasCol -- other text you could search for to find this node
(you can always search for its title; most nodes have none). Writable:
type an alias in and save.
*** <localleader>co : :SkgShowCollectionOverrides
The overriddenCol (the nodes this node overrides -- WRITABLE, shown
even when empty as an "add an override here" surface) and the
overriderCol (the nodes that override this one -- read-only, ☮).
*** <localleader>ch : :SkgShowCollectionHides
The hiderCol and hiddenCol -- BOTH read-only (☮). Hiding is edited from
a subscribee-as-such, never from these cols. On a node that neither
hides nor is hidden, nothing appears.
*** <localleader>cs : :SkgShowCollectionSubscribes
The subscribeeCol (the nodes this node subscribes to -- WRITABLE, shown
even when empty) and the subscriberCol (the nodes that subscribe to
this one -- read-only, ☮).
** <localleader>p : show a path (the backpath through one relation role)
Like the collections, each inserts a request that, on save, grafts the
partners reached through one relation role as inverted, read-only
children, climbing transitively until a fork, cycle, or root. UPPER =
the partner's active (first) role; lower = its passive (second) role.
Every command auto-saves.
*** <localleader>pC : :SkgShowPathsThroughContainers
The nodes that contain this node (its containment ancestry).
*** <localleader>pL : :SkgShowPathsThroughLinkSources
The nodes that link to this node (its textlink sources).
*** <localleader>pl : :SkgShowPathsThroughLinkDests
The nodes this node links to (its textlink dests).
*** <localleader>pO : :SkgShowPathsThroughOverriders
The nodes that override this node.
*** <localleader>po : :SkgShowPathsThroughOverridden
The nodes this node overrides.
*** <localleader>pH : :SkgShowPathsThroughHiders
The nodes that hide this node from their subscriptions.
*** <localleader>ph : :SkgShowPathsThroughHidden
The nodes this node hides from its subscriptions.
*** <localleader>pS : :SkgShowPathsThroughSubscribers
The nodes that subscribe to this node.
*** <localleader>ps : :SkgShowPathsThroughSubscribees
The nodes this node subscribes to.
** <localleader>s : setting node properties
*** first some vocab: 'definitive' and 'indefinitive'
A view of a node can be definitive or indefinitive.
Typically the first place in the buffer that depicts a node
is the definitive view of it. So that other views of that node
cannot contradict it, they are 'indefinitive': Edits to them
cannot affect the node they depict. They are also truncated --
that is, shown without content --
in order to prevent the view from becoming redundant and huge.
*** PITFALL: Don't make something 'definitive' by directly editing its metadata.
Instead, use 'SetDefinitive' (<localleader>sd), below.
That's because Skg has to make other instances of it
(in this same buffer -- and maybe in the future other buffers too)
indefinitive. That's because a buffer can only be saved
if it has at most one definitive instance of a node.
Otherwise, two definitive nodes could contradict each other.

This same caveat does not apply to making a node indefinitive.
While there does exist a command for that ('SetIndefinitive')
you can also 'do it yourself'
by visiting a metadata buffer for the node ('ViewMetadata',
<localleader>vm).
*** <localleader>sd : :SkgSetDefinitive
If you want to see the definitive view of that node
from a place where it is currently shown in an indefinitive form,
call this function. It will make the current definitive view
(if it exists) indefinitive, and then draw a definitive view here.
*** <localleader>si : :SkgSetIndefinitive
This marks a node as 'indefinitive',
as described above.
In brief, it means edits to its title, body or
  (more importantly) children
will not affect it
  (although edits to its children will affect those children,
  unless they are also indefinitive).
*** <localleader>ss : :SkgSetSource
Changes the source of the node at point.
The prompt starts with the current source as editable command-line
text. You can type the new source name directly,
or use S-Left / S-Right to cycle through owned sources;
TAB completes among all configured source names.

The Emacs client's C-? helper (which opened an org buffer listing
every source from inside the minibuffer) has no cmdline analog --
few terminals deliver C-? reliably -- so it's dropped; run
':SkgViewSourceList' beforehand (or afterward) instead. It shows
the same thing: an org buffer listing every configured source name
as a headline, with that source's path as the headline body.

The command only edits the node's metadata in the current buffer.
Save the view afterward (':w') to apply the move.
*** <localleader>sS : :SkgSetSourceRecursive
Like 'SetSource', but it also
changes the source of every content descendent of the node at point
whose source matches the original source of the node at point.
Only descendents for which ParentIs=Affected are traversed.
*** <localleader>sm : :SkgSetMergeRequest
(See [[./merging.org][the docs on merging]] for why would you merge two nodes,
and what it means to do that.)

Run this from the acquirer node.
It prompts for the acquiree, accepting either a bare ID
or an org link like =[[id:ID][label]]=,
and edits the acquirer's metadata to request a merge.

The command only edits the node's metadata in the current buffer.
Save the view afterward to apply the merge.
** <localleader>m : other graph modifications
*** <localleader>mc : :SkgReplaceLinkWithContent
Replace the leaf at point with a content relationship to the
node it links to, then save the buffer.

Point can be on the headline or in the node body. The node at
point can be new or already in the graph. If it already has an
ID, the command warns that the old node might become an orphan.

The headline title plus body must contain exactly one org bracket
link, and that link must be an =id:= link. The node must have no
org-descendents. Its org-parent must be a definitive activeNode
whose source is owned in =skgconfig.toml=.

The command replaces the whole leaf with a same-level
indefinitive activeNode for the link dest. The parent is
definitive, so saving records the dest as content of the
parent; the replacement child is indefinitive, so its own text is
not rewritten from the link label.
*** <localleader>mf : :SkgFork
Fork the node at point that you ALREADY own: create a private
clone that overrides it. This is the explicit counterpart to the
implicit fork (which is triggered by editing a /foreign/ node);
use it to deepen an override chain, e.g. to layer a private =D=
over a public clone =C= that already overrides a foreign =N=.

Refuses if the buffer has unsaved changes ("Save the buffer
before forking."), so the clone's snapshot matches what you see.
Otherwise it stamps the node and auto-saves; the server returns
the usual fork-confirmation buffer (=skg://fork-confirmation=).
There you MUST set the clone's source with 'SetSource'
(<localleader>ss) (it starts as the placeholder PICK-A-SOURCE,
with your config-first owned source suggested above it), then
approve with <localleader>cc; or decline with <localleader>ck
(which also strips the fork request so the next save does not
re-fork). ':w' on the fork-confirmation buffer refuses and points
you back at these two keys.

The clone subscribes to and overrides the node, and the original
keeps its own (owned) content. A node may have at most one clone
you own; forking an already-forked node is rejected, naming the
existing clone. See =docs/forks.md=.
*** <localleader>ml : :SkgReplaceContentWithLink
Replace the branch at point with a link to the node that used to
be there, then save the buffer.

Point can be on the headline or in the node body.
The command uses the headline either way.

The node being replaced must be an existing activeNode with an ID,
and an org-child of a definitive activeNode whose source is
owned in =skgconfig.toml=. The command acts locally,
replacing only this occurrence in the current buffer:
the headline at point and all of its org-descendents,
including scaffolds and phantoms,
are removed from the view and replaced by a same-level headline
whose title is an =[[id:...][...]]= link to the original node.

If the original headline title already contains a link, the
command asks "Are you sure?" before proceeding.
** <localleader>v : view-level commands
*** <localleader>vd : :SkgViewDiffMode
If you use git to manage your data,
enable this view to see what you've changed since your last commit,
i.e. how your worktree differs from HEAD.
Refuses while any skg buffer has unsaved edits.
*** <localleader>ve : :SkgViewNewEmpty
Asks you to pick a data source,
and then provides a root node with that source.
**** why
Every node needs a source.
Children can inherit from their parents,
but to make a root (which has no parents)
you need to choose its source explicitly.
*** <localleader>vh : :SkgHeraldsToggle
This command toggles it.
This toggles whether, at the start of every node,
metadata is shown in its full plain-text glory,
or is instead summarized via brief 'heralds'.
It is turned on at every view by default.
*** <localleader>vl : :SkgLimitSourceSet
Limits display and search to one configured source-set for this
Neovim connection.

The command asks which source-set to activate, with TAB completion
and S-Left / S-Right cycling. It then closes open Skg
buffers after confirmation, and tells the server to use that
source-set for subsequent requests. Choose =all= to remove the
limit.
*** <localleader>vm : :SkgViewMetadata
When called from a node in a view of the graph,
this switches to a view of the metadata of that node,
with one field per org branch. ':w' (or <localleader>cc) commits
the edited tree back into the source headline; ':bd!' cancels;
S-Left / S-Right cycle a field's value (indef, parentIs,
editRequest, source, ...).
It's easy to edit this way.
Note that some properties don't make sense to edit,
like the count of how many contents it has.
(If you want to change how much content a node has,
don't change the node's metadata; change its children in the view.)
*** <localleader>vo : :SkgViewOrgAncestry
If you're at a headline so deep in the tree
that you can't remember where it came from,
this brings you to a new view showing only that ancestry.
This actually works in org-mode outside of skg.
(skg uses org-mode, but org-mode can be used without skg.)
*** <localleader>vr : :SkgReadableIdsToggle
This changes how node IDs are displayed.
The ID itself is a kind of special random gibberish called UUID v4.
When ReadableIdsToggle is on,
only the first 8 characters of the ID are shown (the rest is
concealed to an ellipsis, though the underlying text -- and
copy-paste -- still has the full ID),
and they are followed by the title of the node with that ID,
if Skg can figure it out. Works in .skg file buffers and in
git-status / git-diff buffers alike.
(It doesn't yet know how to read the git history
to find titles for deleted files.)
*** <localleader>vs : :SkgViewIdStack
View and maybe edit the set of links you've stored.
(See "the linkstack" below.)
*** <localleader>vw : :SkgViewWithoutMetadata
If a Visual-mode selection is active (or was just left, via the
='<,'>= marks), opens a new org buffer containing that region,
but with the '(skg ...)' metadata stripped from every headline.
Otherwise it does nothing.
This is useful for copying a read-only projection of some data
into another application.
** <localleader>{i|u|o} : IDs, links and the linkstack
*** some background
**** IDs are mostly invisible, but good to know about.
Every node has an ID,
which you can see if you disable heralds ('HeraldsToggle',
<localleader>vh).
Every link also has an ID and a label,
but all you see when you look at the ID is the label,
unless you disable org-mode (say, by switching to text-mode).
You should never need to see IDs explicitly.
**** The linkstack
is, like it sounds, a stack of links.
A stack is just a list that you interact with,
mostly from one side, the 'top':
You can 'push' something onto the top of the stack,
and you can 'pop' something off of the top of the stack.
But you can also jump to a view of your entire stack,
using (copying, pasting, deleting) it
without any restrictions on order.
*** ]i (also <localleader>in) : :SkgIdNext
Makes the cursor hop among two kinds of IDs:
The ID associated with a node in the view,
and the ID associated with a link in a node's title.
Every node has an ID,
but a title can include multiple or no links.
*** [i (also <localleader>ip) : :SkgIdPrev
See IdNext, above.
*** the stack of links/ids
<localleader>u for push, <localleader>o _ for paste,
<localleader>O _ for pop.

Inside a Vim command-line prompt (search, an Ex command's argument,
...), <localleader> chords aren't available, so paste and pop also
ride cmdline-mode mappings: <C-x>i pastes the top id, <C-x>l pastes
a link to it, <C-x>I pops the id, <C-x>L pops the link. (Push, and
pasting/popping a full node, have no cmdline form -- do those from
a buffer.)
**** <localleader>u : :SkgIdPush
Stores, in the id stack,
a link to the node the cursor is on.
**** <localleader>oi : :SkgPasteId
Inserts the ID at the top of the id stack at the cursor.
Does not modify the stack.
**** <localleader>Oi : :SkgPopId
Like PasteId,
but also removes the node from the stack.
**** <localleader>ol : :SkgPasteLink
Inserts, at the cursor, a link to the node
whose ID is at the top of the id stack.
Does not modify the stack.
**** <localleader>Ol : :SkgPopLink
Like PasteLink,
but also removes the node from the stack.
**** <localleader>on : :SkgPasteNode
This is the easiest way to give a node multiple containers --
push it to the stack, then paste it as a node somewhere else.

Inserts, at the cursor, an indefinitive view
of the node whose ID is at the top of the id stack.
Its metadata includes only the ID and =indef=,
and its title comes from the stack.
Does not modify the stack or save.
Saving will provide the rest of its metadata.
**** <localleader>On : :SkgPopNode
Like PasteNode,
but also removes the node from the stack.
**** <localleader>vs : :SkgViewIdStack
repeated here; described earlier in this doc
** buffer mechanics with no Emacs-chord counterpart
These are defined directly in =attach_content_view=
(nvim/lua/skg/keymaps.lua) rather than translated from a =C-c=
chord: they ride keys nvim already gives special meaning to.
*** <Tab> : toggle the fold at point
Equivalent to org's own =za=: opens or closes the fold under the
cursor.
*** <S-Tab> : toggle global fold visibility
All folds closed <-> all folds open, buffer-wide.
*** ^ : :SkgBeginningOfLine
Toggles the cursor between the start of the line and the start of
the title (the text after the stars and any =(skg ...)= metadata).
The nvim analog of the Emacs client's C-a toggle. On a
non-headline, it's just beginning-of-line.
*** cit / ciT : cycle the TODO keyword
Cycles the headline's TODO keyword forward (=cit=) or backward
(=ciT=) without corrupting its metadata: the =(skg ...)= sexp is
stripped before org's own TODO-cycling runs, then re-inserted
after the stars and before the (possibly new) keyword.
** exporting to org
*** :SkgExportToOrg
Exports Skg data to plain =.org= files, so the data can be read
(and navigated on GitHub) without running Skg. No keybinding.

It asks two things:
- which source-set to export (command-line input with TAB
  completion and S-Left / S-Right cycling); only nodes from that
  set are included. Choose a public-facing source-set so private
  notes are not exported; exporting =all= warns you about this.
- which directory to write to (default =org-exports=). This path
  is interpreted *by the server*, relative to the project root the
  server runs from. Because the server may run inside a Docker
  container whose filesystem differs from the host running Neovim,
  this prompt has no path completion. An absolute path is used
  as-is.

A node is exported when one of its children's title links to the
export-instruction node and that child's body gives a
=target_filepath= (TOML-ish: =target_filepath = docs/setup=,
quoted if it contains whitespace). That parent is written to
=<output-dir>/<target_filepath>.org= as a recursive content view,
stripped of skg metadata. The marker child is omitted.

Links are rewritten to relative org links. A link to another
exported file's root points at that file; a link to a non-root
node points deep, via =[[./file.org::*Heading][label]]=. A nested
export root is shown as a link, not inlined. A link whose dest
is not exported under the chosen set points to a "some links
might be broken" note.

Existing files in the output dir are overwritten in place; other
files there are left untouched. (The same export is available
from the command line for scripting:
=cargo run --bin skg -- export-org [config] [source-set] [output-dir]=.)
** connection, reload, rebuild dbs
Unless you're coding or troubleshooting,
you probably won't need these.
*** :SkgConnectionVerify
Sends a ping to the Rust server
to check if the connection is working.
Displays the result in the echo area.
*** :SkgConnectionEnd
Closes the TCP connection to the Rust server.
*** :SkgRebuildDbs
Rebuilds TypeDB and Tantivy databases,
using the .skg files on disk.
Existing skg views are now invalid; run ':SkgCloseAllSkgBuffers'
to close them.
*** :SkgCloseAllSkgBuffers
This may be warranted after a database rebuild.
Kills every skg view buffer (each triggers a close-view notice to
the server first).
*** :SkgReload
Unloads all skg Lua modules and reloads them from disk.
Useful when you've changed Lua code
and want to pick up the changes without restarting Neovim.
